items.title: Build a lightweight threat model before reading code
items.description: Identify assets, trust boundaries, and the most likely attackers so review time targets the riskiest code paths first.
items.how_to_find: Before reading code, sketch the assets, actors, entry points, and trust boundaries (a quick data-flow diagram). Note what an attacker would target (auth, payments, PII, admin) so the review is risk-driven, not line-by-line.
items.how_to_fix: Document the threat model (STRIDE or simple DFD), agree the highest-risk flows with the team, and focus review effort there. Keep it living — update as the design changes.

items.title: Map all entry points (attack surface)
items.description: Enumerate every place untrusted data enters: HTTP routes, message queues, CLI args, file imports, deserializers, webhooks, and IPC.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate every place untrusted input enters: routes/controllers, message consumers, CLI args, file/upload handlers, webhooks:

```
grep -REn '@(Get|Post|Put|Delete)Mapping|@app.route|router\.(get|post)|addEventListener' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Maintain an inventory of entry points with their auth/authz requirements and input validation, and ensure each is covered by the review + tests. Re-derive after major changes.

references.title: OWASP Attack Surface Analysis Cheat Sheet

items.title: Perform source-to-sink taint analysis
items.description: Trace tainted (user-controlled) data from sources to security-sensitive sinks, noting any sanitizers or encoders along the path.
items.how_to_find: Trace untrusted input (source) to dangerous operations (sink) — DB query, command, file path, HTML, deserializer. Follow the variable through the call graph to see if it's validated/encoded before the sink.
items.how_to_fix: Ensure every source→sink path applies validation/encoding/parameterization at or before the sink. Where feasible, use SAST taint rules to enforce this continuously. Re-verify flagged paths are sanitized.

items.title: Inspect trust boundary crossings
items.description: Wherever data crosses a trust boundary (client to server, service to service, tenant to tenant) validation and authorization must be re-applied server-side.
items.how_to_find: Review each boundary crossing (client→server, service→service, app→DB, app→OS): is input re-validated on the trusted side, and is authz enforced server-side?
items.how_to_fix: Re-validate and authorize on the trusted side of every boundary (never trust client-side checks), and minimize data passed across. Re-review boundary handling.

items.title: Prioritize security-relevant diffs in PR reviews
items.description: In incremental reviews, focus on changes touching auth, crypto, parsers, queries, and dependency updates rather than reviewing line-by-line uniformly.
items.how_to_find:
In PRs, focus on changes touching auth, crypto, input handling, deserialization, queries, and dependencies:

```
git diff --stat ; git diff -- '*auth*' '*crypto*' '*query*'
```
items.how_to_fix: Flag security-sensitive files for mandatory security review (CODEOWNERS), require tests for security changes, and block risky patterns in CI. Re-review such diffs closely.

items.title: Configure SAST with project-appropriate rulesets
items.description: Stand up a SAST engine (Semgrep, CodeQL, SonarQube) tuned to the languages and frameworks in use, and run it in CI on every change.
items.how_to_find:
Run SAST with language/framework rules:

```
semgrep --config=auto .
# or: codeql, sonarqube, bandit (py), gosec (go), brakeman (rails)
```
items.how_to_fix: Adopt SAST in CI with curated rulesets, fail builds on high-severity findings, tune out false positives with justifications, and keep rules updated. Re-scan on every PR.

items.title: Grep for dangerous sink patterns
items.description: Use ripgrep to quickly locate high-risk APIs: exec/system, raw SQL, eval, deserializers, innerHTML, and disabled TLS verification.
items.how_to_find:
Quick-grep for high-risk sinks:

```
grep -REn 'eval\(|exec\(|system\(|innerHTML|pickle.loads|ObjectInputStream|Runtime.getRuntime' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Treat each hit as a review target; replace with safe alternatives (parameterization, safe APIs, encoding). Encode these patterns as SAST/CI rules so they're caught automatically. Re-grep.

items.title: Write custom rules for project-specific sinks
items.description: Encode internal dangerous helpers (custom query builders, in-house exec wrappers) as Semgrep/CodeQL rules so they are caught automatically.
items.how_to_find:
Identify internal helper functions that reach dangerous sinks (e.g. a `db.raw()` wrapper) and write Semgrep/CodeQL rules for them:

```
# semgrep rule matching your internal unsafe wrapper -> require sanitized arg
```
items.how_to_fix: Maintain custom SAST rules for project-specific sinks/wrappers, run them in CI, and update as the codebase evolves. Re-scan with the custom ruleset.

items.title: Triage SAST findings and suppress with justification
items.description: Investigate findings for exploitability and false positives; suppress only with a documented reason, never blanket-disable rules.
items.how_to_find:
Review SAST output, confirm true/false positives, and check that suppressions have documented justifications:

```
grep -REn 'nosec|nosemgrep|codeql\[.*\]|SuppressWarnings' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Require a written justification + reviewer approval for every suppression, periodically re-audit suppressions, and prefer fixing over suppressing. Re-review suppressions.

items.title: Diff against history to spot weakened controls
items.description: Compare versions to detect removed validation, downgraded crypto, new debug flags, or silently added dependencies.
items.how_to_find:
Look for commits that remove/relax security controls:

```
git log -p -S 'verify=' -- '*.py' ; git log -p --pickaxe-regex -S 'PermitRootLogin|csrf|sanitize'
```
items.how_to_fix: Flag any diff that removes validation/auth/crypto checks for mandatory review, and add tests locking in the control. Re-review history for weakened controls.

items.title: Detect string-concatenated SQL queries
items.description: Queries assembled by concatenating or interpolating user input into the SQL string are classic SQLi sinks.
items.how_to_find:
Find SQL built by concatenation/interpolation:

```
grep -REn '(SELECT|INSERT|UPDATE|DELETE).*(\+|%|\$\{|f")' src/
```

Query strings containing user input via concat/format = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries / prepared statements everywhere (bind variables), never string-build SQL with user input. Re-scan and add a SAST rule to block concatenated SQL.

references.title: OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet

items.title: Review ORM raw query and rawSQL escape hatches
items.description: ORMs are safe by default but expose raw query methods (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Find raw ORM escape hatches:

```
grep -REn '\.raw\(|rawSQL|createNativeQuery|@Query|executeRawSql|db.execute\(' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Parameterize raw queries (bound params, not string interpolation), prefer the ORM's safe query builder, and review each raw usage. Re-scan.

references.title: OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet

items.title: Check dynamic table/column identifiers and ORDER BY
items.description: Parameterization does not cover identifiers (table/column names, ORDER BY, LIMIT); these must be allowlisted, not bound.
items.how_to_find:
Find dynamic identifiers (can't be parameterized) from user input:

```
grep -REn 'ORDER BY.*\+|table\s*=\s*|column\s*=\s*req' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Allowlist dynamic identifiers/sort columns against a fixed set of known-good values (never interpolate raw input), and map user input to safe internal names. Re-review dynamic identifiers.

references.title: OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet

items.title: Detect NoSQL and ODM injection
items.description: NoSQL databases (MongoDB) are vulnerable when raw query objects, $where, or where() accept attacker-controlled operators.
items.how_to_find:
Find NoSQL queries built from raw objects/operators:

```
grep -REn '\$where|\.find\(req\.|\$regex|mapReduce' src/
```

Passing raw user objects into Mongo queries allows operator injection.
items.how_to_fix: Validate/cast input types, reject query operators (`$`-prefixed keys) from user input, use schema validation, and parameterize. Re-review NoSQL query construction.

references.title: CWE-943: Improper Neutralization in a Data Query

items.title: Audit stored procedures for dynamic SQL
items.description: Parameterized calls to stored procedures are safe only if the procedure itself does not build dynamic SQL from its parameters.
items.how_to_find:
Review stored procs for dynamic SQL execution:

```
# search DB for EXEC(@sql) / EXECUTE IMMEDITE / sp_executesql with concatenation
```
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized `sp_executesql`/bind variables inside procs (never concatenate), validate inputs, and least-privilege the proc's DB account. Re-review procs.

references.title: OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet

sections.title: Injection Sinks — OS Command Injection Sinks

items.title: Flag shell-interpreted command execution
items.description: Calls like system(), os.system, exec with shell=True, Runtime.exec("sh -c ..."), or child_process.exec invoke a shell and are injectable.
items.how_to_find:
Find shell-interpreted exec with user input:

```
grep -REn 'os.system|subprocess.*shell=True|exec\(|`.*\$|child_process.exec\(|Runtime.getRuntime' src/
```

Shell string built from input = critical (command injection).
items.how_to_fix: Use argument-array execution without a shell (e.g. `subprocess.run([...], shell=False)`, `execFile`), avoid shell metacharacters, and validate/allowlist arguments. Re-scan.

references.title: OWASP OS Command Injection Defense Cheat Sheet

items.title: Prefer argument-array execution without a shell
items.description: Even array-based exec is risky if the program/path is attacker-controlled or arguments allow option injection (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether exec calls pass an argv array vs a single shell string:

```
grep -REn 'exec\(|spawn\(|Popen\(' src/   # inspect arg form
```
items.how_to_fix: Pass commands as an argument array (no shell), avoid `shell=True`/`exec` string forms, and never interpolate user input into the command. Re-review exec calls.

references.title: OWASP OS Command Injection Defense Cheat Sheet

items.title: Audit per-language process APIs
items.description: Review Python subprocess/os, Node child_process, Java ProcessBuilder/Runtime, Ruby Kernel#system/backticks, Go os/exec, and PHP exec/passthru/proc_open.
items.how_to_find:
Review the language's process APIs for unsafe usage:

```
# py: os.system/popen/subprocess ; node: child_process ; java: Runtime/ProcessBuilder ; go: exec.Command; php: exec/shell_exec/system/passthru
grep -REn 'shell_exec|passthru|proc_open|popen|ProcessBuilder' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Use the safe form of each API (argv arrays, no shell), validate inputs, and least-privilege the process. Re-review each process invocation.

items.title: Check indirect command execution via templates and config
items.description: Build scripts, Makefiles, CI YAML, and templating that interpolate input into shell commands are command-injection sinks too.
items.how_to_find:
Look for indirect execution (templates, config, deserialization invoking commands):

```
grep -REn 'Runtime|ProcessBuilder|os\.|subprocess' templates/ config/ src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Treat template/config-driven command execution as a sink: restrict what can be executed, allowlist commands, and don't let untrusted data reach these paths. Re-review.

references.title: OWASP OS Command Injection Defense Cheat Sheet

items.title: Detect file paths built from user input
items.description: Concatenating request data into a file path enables ../ traversal to read or write outside the intended directory.
items.how_to_find:
Find file operations using user input:

```
grep -REn 'open\(|readFile|File\(|Paths.get|fs\.(read|write)' src/ | grep -iE 'req|param|input|user'
```
items.how_to_fix: Resolve to a canonical path and verify it stays within an allowed base directory before use; prefer opaque IDs mapped to server-side paths over raw filenames. Re-review file access.

items.title: Verify canonicalization happens before the boundary check
items.description: Checks must resolve symlinks and normalize (realpath/Path.normalize) and then verify the result is still within the allowed root.
items.how_to_find:
Check the order: is the path canonicalized (realpath) BEFORE the allowed-directory check?

```
grep -REn 'realpath|canonicalize|normalize|Path.*resolve' src/
```

Checking before canonicalization allows `../` bypass.
items.how_to_fix: Canonicalize first (resolve symlinks/`..`), then assert the result is within the allowed base (prefix check on the canonical path). Re-review the check ordering.

items.title: Account for encoding and null-byte bypasses
items.description: Naive blocklists for ../ are bypassed via URL/double/Unicode encoding or null bytes; rely on canonicalization, not filtering.
items.how_to_find: Check for decoding of input (URL/unicode) and null-byte handling before path checks.
items.how_to_fix: Decode/normalize input once, reject null bytes and unexpected encodings, then canonicalize and validate. Use platform-safe path APIs. Re-review decoding.

items.title: Check archive extraction for Zip Slip
items.description: Extracting zip/tar entries using their stored names allows ../ entries to write outside the target directory (Zip Slip).
items.how_to_find:
Find archive extraction that writes entry names directly:

```
grep -REn 'ZipEntry|getName\(\)|extractall|tarfile|unzip' src/
```

Writing `entry.getName()` without validating the target = Zip Slip.
items.how_to_fix: Validate each extracted entry's resolved target stays within the destination dir (canonical prefix check), reject `..`/absolute paths, and cap size/count. Re-review extraction.

items.title: Detect Java native deserialization (ObjectInputStream)
items.description: readObject on attacker data with vulnerable gadget classes on the classpath leads to RCE.
items.how_to_find:
Find Java native deserialization:

```
grep -REn 'ObjectInputStream|readObject\(|readUnshared|XMLDecoder' src/
```

Deserializing untrusted bytes = critical RCE.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid native serialization for untrusted data; use JSON with strict schemas. If unavoidable, use a strict allowlist (`ObjectInputFilter`) of expected classes and keep libraries patched. Re-scan.

items.title: Detect Python pickle/yaml.load on untrusted data
items.description: pickle.loads, yaml.load (without SafeLoader), and shelve/jsonpickle on untrusted input execute arbitrary code.
items.how_to_find:
Find pickle / unsafe YAML:

```
grep -REn 'pickle.loads|pickle.load|yaml.load\(|jsonpickle' src/
```

`pickle`/`yaml.load` on untrusted input = critical RCE.
items.how_to_fix: Never unpickle untrusted data; use JSON. For YAML use `yaml.safe_load`. Validate against a schema. Re-scan.

items.title: Detect PHP unserialize() on untrusted input
items.description: unserialize() of attacker data triggers magic methods (__wakeup/__destruct) enabling POP-chain exploitation.
items.how_to_find:
Find PHP unserialize:

```
grep -REn 'unserialize\(' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Avoid `unserialize()` on untrusted input; use `json_decode`. If required, pass `['allowed_classes' => false]` and validate. Re-scan.

items.title: Detect .NET unsafe deserializers
items.description: BinaryFormatter, LosFormatter, NetDataContractSerializer, and TypeNameHandling.All in Json.NET deserialize types unsafely.
items.how_to_find:
Find unsafe .NET deserializers:

```
grep -REn 'BinaryFormatter|LosFormatter|NetDataContractSerializer|TypeNameHandling' src/
```

`BinaryFormatter` / `TypeNameHandling.All` = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Stop using `BinaryFormatter`/`NetDataContractSerializer`; use `System.Text.Json`/`DataContractJsonSerializer` with no type-name handling. Re-scan.

items.title: Detect Ruby Marshal.load and YAML.load
items.description: Marshal.load and Psych YAML.load on untrusted data can instantiate arbitrary objects leading to RCE.
items.how_to_find:
Find Ruby unsafe deserialization:

```
grep -REn 'Marshal.load|YAML.load\(|Psych.load\(' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Avoid `Marshal.load` on untrusted data; use `YAML.safe_load` / JSON with allowlisted classes. Re-scan.

sections.title: Injection Sinks — Template Injection & Dynamic Eval

items.title: Detect server-side template injection (SSTI)
items.description: Rendering user input as a template (Jinja2, Twig, Freemarker, Velocity, ERB, Handlebars) instead of as data leads to RCE.
items.how_to_find:
Find user input rendered as a template (not just as data):

```
grep -REn 'render_template_string|Template\(|new Function|Handlebars.compile|freemarker|velocity' src/
```

User input concatenated into template source = critical SSTI.
items.how_to_fix: Never build templates from user input; pass user data as template variables/context only, use sandboxed/logic-less templates, and validate. Re-review template rendering.

references.title: CWE-1336: Improper Neutralization in a Template Engine

items.title: Detect dynamic code evaluation (eval/exec)
items.description: eval, exec, Function(), setTimeout(string), compile, and runtime code generation on user input execute arbitrary code.
items.how_to_find:
Find dynamic code evaluation:

```
grep -REn '\beval\(|\bexec\(|new Function\(|setTimeout\(".*"|Function\(' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove `eval`/`exec`/`new Function` on user input; use safe parsers/dispatch tables/allowlists instead. Re-scan.

items.title: Check expression-language and SpEL injection
items.description: Spring SpEL, OGNL, MVEL, and similar expression evaluators on user input enable code execution.
items.how_to_find:
Find expression-language evaluation of user input:

```
grep -REn 'SpelExpressionParser|MVEL|OGNL|EvaluationContext|#\{' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Don't evaluate user-controlled expressions; if EL is required, use a restricted/sandboxed context and allowlist. Re-review EL usage.

items.title: Check dynamic module loading and reflection
items.description: require/import, Class.forName, getattr, and reflection driven by user input can load and invoke unexpected code paths.
items.how_to_find:
Find dynamic class/module loading from input:

```
grep -REn 'Class.forName|importlib.import_module|require\(.*req|getattr\(|reflect' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Allowlist which classes/modules can be loaded (map user input to fixed values), avoid reflection on untrusted names, and validate. Re-review dynamic loading.

items.title: Detect endpoints missing authentication
items.description: Routes that should require login but lack a guard/middleware/decorator are unauthenticated access points.
items.how_to_find:
Compare routes against auth middleware/annotations to find unauthenticated endpoints:

```
grep -REn '@(Get|Post)Mapping|@app.route|router\.' src/   # cross-check each for an auth guard
```
items.how_to_fix: Apply authentication by default (global middleware/deny-by-default) and explicitly annotate the few public routes, with tests asserting protected routes require auth. Re-audit routes.

references.title: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

items.title: Verify password/token comparison is constant-time
items.description: Comparing secrets with == leaks length/timing; use constant-time comparison, and verify passwords via slow KDFs.
items.how_to_find:
Find non-constant-time comparisons of secrets/tokens:

```
grep -REn '==.*(token|hmac|signature|secret)|\.equals\(.*token' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Use constant-time comparison (`hmac.compare_digest`, `crypto.timingSafeEqual`, `MessageDigest.isEqual`) for tokens/MACs/signatures. Re-review comparisons.

items.title: Verify password hashing uses a strong KDF
items.description: Passwords must be stored with bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2 with appropriate cost — never plaintext or fast hashes.
items.how_to_find:
Find how passwords are hashed:

```
grep -REn 'bcrypt|scrypt|argon2|pbkdf2|MessageDigest|sha256\(|md5\(' src/
```

Fast hashes (MD5/SHA) for passwords = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Hash passwords with a memory-hard KDF (argon2id preferred, or bcrypt/scrypt/PBKDF2) with proper cost + per-user salt; migrate legacy hashes on next login. Re-review hashing.

references.title: CWE-916: Use of Password Hash With Insufficient Computational Effort

items.title: Check brute-force protection on auth endpoints
items.description: Login, OTP, and password-reset endpoints need rate limiting and lockout to resist credential stuffing and brute force.
items.how_to_find:
Check login/OTP endpoints for rate limiting/lockout:

```
grep -REn 'rate.?limit|throttle|lockout|attempts' src/
```

No throttling on auth = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Add rate limiting + progressive lockout + CAPTCHA/step-up on auth endpoints, monitor for spraying, and enforce MFA. Re-test brute-force resistance.

references.title: CWE-307: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts

items.title: Look for authentication logic bypasses
items.description: Flawed boolean logic, early returns, default-allow branches, or unverified "remember me" flows can bypass authentication.
items.how_to_find:
Review auth flows for logic flaws (early returns, `||` defaults, unverified state, order-of-checks):

```
grep -REn 'if\s*\(.*isAdmin|return true|auth\s*=\s*true|bypass|debug' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Fail closed, centralize auth decisions, verify all conditions server-side, and add tests for bypass cases (missing/invalid/expired credentials). Re-review the logic.

items.title: Detect missing object-level authorization (IDOR)
items.description: Handlers that fetch a resource by id from input without verifying ownership allow access to other users records.
items.how_to_find:
Find handlers that fetch objects by user-supplied ID without an ownership check:

```
grep -REn 'findById\(|get\(.*id\)|params\.id|where.*id' src/   # verify each checks ownership/tenant
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce object-level authorization on every access (check the object belongs to the caller/tenant), prefer scoped queries (`where owner_id = :me`), and add IDOR tests. Re-review object access.

references.title: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

items.title: Detect missing function-level authorization
items.description: Admin/privileged actions must check role/permission server-side, not rely on hidden UI or client-side gating.
items.how_to_find:
Find privileged actions lacking role checks:

```
grep -REn 'admin|delete|approve|role|permission' src/   # cross-check for authz guards
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce role/permission checks on every privileged function (deny-by-default, centralized), and test that lower-privileged users are rejected. Re-audit privileged handlers.

items.title: Check for mass assignment / over-posting
items.description: Binding request bodies directly to models lets attackers set protected fields (isAdmin, role, balance).
items.how_to_find:
Find binding of request bodies directly to models:

```
grep -REn 'Object.assign\(|\.\.\.req.body|@ModelAttribute|update\(req.body|params.permit' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Bind only explicitly allowed fields (allowlist DTOs / strong params), never bind sensitive fields (role, isAdmin, price) from input, and validate. Re-review binding.

references.title: CWE-915: Improperly Controlled Modification of Object Attributes

items.title: Verify tenant isolation in multi-tenant queries
items.description: Every data query in a multi-tenant system must be scoped by tenant id to prevent cross-tenant data exposure.
items.how_to_find:
Check that every query filters by tenant:

```
grep -REn 'SELECT|find\(|where\(' src/   # confirm a tenant_id/org_id predicate
```

Missing tenant scoping = critical cross-tenant data access.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce tenant scoping on every query (row-level security / mandatory tenant predicate / scoped repository), derive tenant from the session not the request body, and add cross-tenant tests. Re-audit queries.

items.title: Confirm authorization is centralized and deny-by-default
items.description: Scattered, ad-hoc permission checks are error-prone; a central policy layer that defaults to deny is more reliable.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether authz is scattered ad-hoc vs centralized:

```
grep -REn 'can\(|authorize\(|@PreAuthorize|policy|guard' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Centralize authorization (policy/guard layer), default to deny, and make each resource declare its required permission. Add tests. Re-review authz architecture.

sections.title: AuthN & AuthZ Flaws — Session & Token Handling

items.title: Verify session cookie security attributes
items.description: Session cookies must set HttpOnly, Secure, and an appropriate SameSite to resist theft and CSRF.
items.how_to_find:
Check cookie flags:

```
grep -REn 'Set-Cookie|cookie\(|SameSite|HttpOnly|Secure' src/
```

Missing `HttpOnly`/`Secure`/`SameSite` on session cookies = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, and `SameSite=Lax/Strict` on session cookies, scope path/domain tightly, and use short lifetimes. Re-review cookie config.

references.title: CWE-1004: Sensitive Cookie Without HttpOnly Flag

items.title: Check session regeneration on privilege change
items.description: Sessions must be regenerated on login and privilege elevation to prevent session fixation.
items.how_to_find:
Check that the session ID is rotated on login/privilege change:

```
grep -REn 'regenerate|session.*renew|new session|rotate' src/
```

No regeneration on login = session fixation risk.
items.how_to_fix: Regenerate the session identifier on login and privilege elevation, and invalidate the old one. Add a test. Re-review the login flow.

items.title: Verify JWT signature and algorithm validation
items.description: JWT verification must enforce a fixed algorithm and reject alg=none; never trust unsigned or attacker-chosen-algorithm tokens.
items.how_to_find:
Check JWT verification for alg confusion / `none`:

```
grep -REn 'jwt.decode|verify\(|algorithms|alg|none' src/
```

Accepting `alg:none` or not pinning the algorithm = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Verify the signature with a pinned algorithm/key (reject `none` and unexpected algs), validate `exp`/`aud`/`iss`, and never trust the header's alg. Re-review JWT handling.

references.title: OWASP JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet
CWE-347: Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature

items.title: Check token expiry, revocation, and refresh handling
items.description: Tokens need bounded lifetimes, server-side revocation for stateful needs (logout, compromise), and safe refresh rotation.
items.how_to_find:
Review token lifetimes and revocation:

```
grep -REn 'exp|expires|refresh|revoke|blacklist' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Use short-lived access tokens + rotating refresh tokens, support server-side revocation, and invalidate on logout/password change. Re-review token lifecycle.

references.title: OWASP JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet

items.title: Verify session/token identifiers are unpredictable
items.description: Session ids and reset tokens must come from a CSPRNG with sufficient entropy, not sequential ids or weak RNG.
items.how_to_find:
Check how session/token IDs are generated:

```
grep -REn 'Math.random|new Random|uuid|SecureRandom|token\s*=' src/
```

Predictable/sequential IDs = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Generate identifiers from a CSPRNG with sufficient entropy (128-bit+), avoid predictable schemes, and use the framework's secure session store. Re-review ID generation.

references.title: CWE-330: Use of Insufficiently Random Values

items.title: Detect secrets committed in source code
items.description: API keys, passwords, private keys, and tokens hardcoded in code are exposed to anyone with repo access.
items.how_to_find:
Scan the tree for secrets:

```
gitleaks detect --source . -v
grep -REn 'api[_-]?key|secret|password|AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}|-----BEGIN' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove secrets from code, move them to a secrets manager / env config, rotate anything exposed, and add a secret-scanning pre-commit hook + CI gate. Re-scan.

items.title: Check config files and committed env files for secrets
items.description: Secrets in application.properties, appsettings.json, .env, or Helm/Terraform files frequently get committed by mistake.
items.how_to_find:
Look for secrets in config/env files:

```
grep -REn 'password|secret|token|key' --include='*.env' --include='*.yml' --include='*.properties' .
git ls-files | grep -E '\.env$|credentials'
```
items.how_to_fix: Keep secrets out of committed config; use `.env` (gitignored) + a secrets manager, template example configs without values, and rotate exposed secrets. Re-scan.

references.title: CWE-256: Plaintext Storage of a Password

items.title: Scan git history for previously committed secrets
items.description: Removing a secret from HEAD does not remove it from history; leaked secrets must be rotated, not just deleted.
items.how_to_find:
Secrets removed from HEAD may persist in history:

```
gitleaks detect --source . --log-opts="--all"
trufflehog git file://. 
```
items.how_to_fix: Rotate any secret ever committed (assume compromised), purge from history (git filter-repo/BFG) if required, and add scanning to CI. Re-scan history.

items.title: Check client bundles and mobile apps for embedded secrets
items.description: Secrets shipped in frontend JS bundles or mobile binaries are public; only public/anon keys belong client-side.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect shipped client artifacts:

```
grep -REn 'api[_-]?key|secret|firebase|AIza[0-9A-Za-z_-]{35}' dist/ build/
strings app.apk | grep -iE 'key|secret'
```
items.how_to_fix: Never ship secrets to clients (they're extractable); move them server-side behind authenticated APIs, use short-lived tokens, and rotate. Re-scan bundles.

items.title: Detect weak hash algorithms (MD5/SHA1)
items.description: MD5 and SHA1 are broken for integrity/signatures and unsuitable for passwords; flag their security use.
items.how_to_find:
Find weak hashes:

```
grep -REn 'MD5|SHA-?1|md5\(|sha1\(' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Use SHA-256+ for integrity and a KDF (argon2/bcrypt) for passwords; remove MD5/SHA1 for security purposes. Re-scan.

references.title: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

items.title: Detect insecure cipher modes (ECB) and missing auth
items.description: ECB leaks patterns and unauthenticated CBC is malleable; prefer authenticated modes like AES-GCM.
items.how_to_find:
Find ECB / unauthenticated modes:

```
grep -REn 'ECB|AES/CBC|Cipher.getInstance\("AES"\)|createCipher\(' src/
```

ECB or CBC-without-MAC = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Use authenticated encryption (AES-GCM / ChaCha20-Poly1305), never ECB, and prefer high-level libs (libsodium/Tink). Re-scan.

references.title: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

items.title: Detect static/reused IVs and nonces
items.description: A constant or reused IV/nonce destroys the security of stream ciphers and GCM; nonces must be unique per encryption.
items.how_to_find:
Find hardcoded/reused IVs:

```
grep -REn 'IvParameterSpec|GCMParameterSpec|iv\s*=\s*("|b")|new byte\[16\]' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Generate a fresh random IV/nonce per encryption (critical for GCM/CTR), never reuse with the same key, and transmit it alongside the ciphertext. Re-review IV handling.

references.title: CWE-329: Generation of Predictable IV with CBC Mode

items.title: Detect weak random number generation for secrets
items.description: Math.random, java.util.Random, rand(), and mt_rand are not cryptographically secure for tokens, keys, or IVs.
items.how_to_find:
Find non-CSPRNG used for security values:

```
grep -REn 'Math.random|new Random\(|rand\(\)|mt_rand' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Use a CSPRNG (`secrets`, `crypto.randomBytes`, `SecureRandom`) for tokens/keys/IVs/salts. Re-scan.

references.title: CWE-338: Use of Cryptographically Weak PRNG

items.title: Review key management and key derivation
items.description: Keys derived from low-entropy material, reused across purposes, or hardcoded undermine otherwise sound crypto.
items.how_to_find:
Check how keys are stored/derived/rotated:

```
grep -REn 'key\s*=|deriveKey|PBKDF2|KMS|KeyStore|getenv' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Store keys in a KMS/HSM/secret store (not code), derive with a proper KDF + salt, separate keys per purpose, and support rotation. Re-review key management.

items.title: Detect disabled TLS certificate verification
items.description: Disabling verification (verify=False, rejectUnauthorized:false, InsecureSkipVerify, trust-all managers) enables MITM.
items.how_to_find:
Find disabled cert verification:

```
grep -REn 'verify=False|rejectUnauthorized:\s*false|InsecureSkipVerify|ALLOW_ALL|trustAllCerts' src/
```

Disabled verification = high (MITM).
items.how_to_fix: Enable certificate validation with the default trust store, remove `verify=False`/`InsecureSkipVerify`, and fail closed on errors. Re-scan and test with an invalid cert.

references.title: OWASP Transport Layer Security Cheat Sheet

items.title: Verify hostname verification is enabled
items.description: Validating the chain but not the hostname still permits MITM with any valid certificate; hostname checks must be on.
items.how_to_find:
Find disabled hostname verification:

```
grep -REn 'HostnameVerifier|ALLOW_ALL_HOSTNAME|verify_mode|check_hostname\s*=\s*False' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable hostname verification (default verifiers), don't override with allow-all, and validate the hostname matches the cert. Re-review.

references.title: CWE-297: Improper Validation of Certificate with Host Mismatch
OWASP Transport Layer Security Cheat Sheet

items.title: Check for outdated TLS versions and weak ciphers
items.description: Forcing SSLv3/TLS 1.0/1.1 or enabling weak cipher suites weakens confidentiality; require TLS 1.2+.
items.how_to_find:
Find pinned-old TLS / weak ciphers in code:

```
grep -REn 'SSLv3|TLSv1(\.0|\.1)?|ssl_version|PROTOCOL_TLSv1' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Require TLS 1.2+ (prefer 1.3), use the library default cipher suites, and remove hardcoded weak protocols/ciphers. Re-scan.

references.title: OWASP Transport Layer Security Cheat Sheet

items.title: Review certificate pinning implementation (mobile)
items.description: Mobile and high-assurance clients may pin certificates; broken or bypassable pinning gives false confidence.
items.how_to_find:
Check pinning code for correctness and backup pins:

```
grep -REn 'CertificatePinner|pin-set|TrustKit|PinningTrustManager' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Implement pinning with backup pins + a rotation plan, fail closed, and combine with tamper detection. Re-review pinning.

references.title: OWASP Certificate and Public Key Pinning

sections.title: Input / Output Handling — XSS Output Sinks

items.title: Detect unencoded output in HTML context
items.description: Writing user input into HTML without context-appropriate encoding produces reflected or stored XSS.
items.how_to_find:
Find user data written to HTML without encoding:

```
grep -REn 'render\(|response.write|echo |print\(.*req|{!!|\|safe|autoescape\s*false' src/ templates/
```
items.how_to_fix: Use context-aware output encoding (framework auto-escaping on), never disable escaping for user data, and treat all output as untrusted. Re-review templates.

references.title: OWASP Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet

items.title: Detect innerHTML and DOM XSS sinks
items.description: Assigning user input to innerHTML, outerHTML, document.write, or insertAdjacentHTML executes injected markup.
items.how_to_find:
Find DOM XSS sinks:

```
grep -REn 'innerHTML|outerHTML|document.write|insertAdjacentHTML|\.html\(' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Use safe DOM APIs (`textContent`, `setAttribute`), avoid `innerHTML` with untrusted data, and sanitize with DOMPurify when HTML is required. Re-review DOM sinks.

references.title: OWASP DOM based XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet

items.title: Audit dangerouslySetInnerHTML and v-html usage
items.description: Framework escape hatches (React dangerouslySetInnerHTML, Vue v-html, Angular bypassSecurityTrust) reintroduce XSS.
items.how_to_find:
Find framework raw-HTML escape hatches:

```
grep -REn 'dangerouslySetInnerHTML|v-html|\[innerHTML\]|ng-bind-html' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Avoid raw-HTML bindings for untrusted data; sanitize with DOMPurify if unavoidable, and prefer text bindings. Re-review each usage.

references.title: OWASP Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet

items.title: Check attribute and URL context injection
items.description: User input in href/src or event-handler attributes enables javascript: URLs and attribute-breaking XSS.
items.how_to_find:
Find user input in attribute/URL/JS contexts:

```
grep -REn 'href=.*\+|src=.*\+|javascript:|style=.*\+|on\w+=' src/ templates/
```
items.how_to_fix: Encode per context (attribute/URL/JS), allowlist URL schemes (block `javascript:`), and avoid building event handlers/styles from input. Re-review.

references.title: OWASP Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet

items.title: Detect server-side fetch of user-supplied URLs
items.description: Fetching a URL from user input lets attackers reach internal services and cloud metadata endpoints.
items.how_to_find:
Find server-side requests using user input:

```
grep -REn 'requests.get\(|urllib|fetch\(|axios\.|HttpClient|curl_exec|OkHttp' src/ | grep -iE 'req|param|url|input'
```
items.how_to_fix: Validate/allowlist destination hosts, resolve + block internal/metadata ranges, and fetch only via a constrained client. Re-review outbound fetch.

items.title: Verify destination allowlisting and metadata blocking
items.description: Defenses should allowlist destinations and block link-local/private/metadata ranges (169.254.169.254) after DNS resolution.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether outbound requests block internal/link-local/metadata targets:

```
grep -REn '169.254.169.254|metadata|127.0.0.1|localhost|10\.|192.168' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Allowlist permitted destinations, deny RFC1918/link-local/metadata (169.254.169.254) after DNS resolution, and enforce at egress. Re-test SSRF to internal targets is blocked.

items.title: Check for redirect-following and DNS rebinding gaps
items.description: Allowlist checks before a request can be bypassed by redirects to internal hosts or DNS rebinding (TOCTOU on resolution).
items.how_to_find:
Check redirect handling + whether the resolved IP is re-validated (TOCTOU/DNS rebinding):

```
grep -REn 'allow_redirects|followRedirects|maxRedirects' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable or tightly limit redirects, re-validate the final resolved IP against the allowlist (pin the resolved IP), and set timeouts. Re-test rebinding/redirect bypass.

items.title: Audit indirect SSRF via parsers and integrations
items.description: PDF/HTML/image converters, webhooks, XML external entities, and URL preview features are common SSRF entry points.
items.how_to_find:
Look for SSRF via parsers/integrations (PDF/image/XML fetchers, webhooks, url previews):

```
grep -REn 'ImageMagick|wkhtmltopdf|SSRF|webhook|preview|xinclude' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Apply the same allowlist/egress controls to parser/integration fetches, disable remote resource loading in parsers, and sandbox them. Re-review indirect fetch paths.

sections.title: Input / Output Handling — File Upload Handling

items.title: Verify upload content-type and magic-byte validation
items.description: Relying on the client-supplied Content-Type or extension allows uploading executable or malicious content.
items.how_to_find:
Check how uploads are validated (extension vs content):

```
grep -REn 'content-type|mimetype|multipart|getOriginalFilename|file.type' src/
```

Trusting the client content-type/extension = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Validate by magic bytes/content sniffing against an allowlist (not just extension/header), re-encode images where possible, and reject mismatches. Re-review validation.

references.title: CWE-434: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type

items.title: Check upload filename and storage path handling
items.description: Using the client filename to build the storage path enables traversal and overwriting of sensitive files.
items.how_to_find:
Check filename handling for traversal/overwrite:

```
grep -REn 'getOriginalFilename|filename|path.join.*upload|save\(' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Generate server-side random filenames (ignore client name), store outside the webroot, and validate the resolved path stays in the upload dir. Re-review storage.

references.title: CWE-434: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type

items.title: Verify uploads cannot be executed by the server
items.description: Uploads stored in a web-executable directory (or interpreted by the app server) can lead to RCE via uploaded scripts.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether the upload dir can execute code (php/jsp/aspx):

```
grep -REn 'upload' config/ nginx*/ .htaccess 2>/dev/null   # check exec handlers on the dir
```

Executable uploads = critical RCE.
items.how_to_fix: Store uploads outside the webroot / on object storage, disable script execution in the upload dir (web-server config), serve via a controlled handler with `Content-Disposition`, and allowlist types. Re-test that an uploaded script won't execute.

references.title: CWE-434: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type

items.title: Check size limits, decompression, and malware scanning
items.description: Missing size limits enable DoS, nested archives cause zip bombs, and unscanned files can distribute malware.
items.how_to_find:
Check for size/decompression limits and AV scanning:

```
grep -REn 'maxFileSize|MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH|unzip|gzip|clamav' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce size limits, guard against decompression bombs (limit ratio/output size), and scan uploads with AV before use. Re-review limits.

references.title: CWE-409: Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data

sections.title: Input / Output Handling — XXE & XML Parser Sinks

items.title: Detect XML parsers that resolve external entities
items.description: Parsers that process external entities on untrusted XML enable file disclosure, SSRF, and DoS (billion laughs).
items.how_to_find:
Find XML parsing that may resolve external entities:

```
grep -REn 'DocumentBuilder|SAXParser|XMLReader|etree|libxml|XmlReader|DOMParser' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable DTDs/external entities on every parser (`disallow-doctype-decl`, `external-general-entities=false`, `resolve_entities=False`), and prefer JSON. Re-review parser config.

references.title: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference

items.title: Audit per-language XML parser hardening
items.description: Java (DocumentBuilderFactory/SAX), .NET (XmlReader/XmlDocument), Python (lxml/xml), and PHP (libxml) all need explicit hardening.
items.how_to_find:
Review each language's XML config:

```
# java: setFeature disallow-doctype ; py: defusedxml ; .NET: XmlReaderSettings.DtdProcessing=Prohibit ; php: libxml_disable_entity_loader
grep -REn 'DtdProcessing|disallow-doctype|defusedxml|libxml_disable_entity_loader' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Apply the hardened configuration for each parser (or use `defusedxml`-style safe libraries), and centralize XML parsing behind a safe helper. Re-review.

references.title: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference

items.title: Check entity-expansion and DoS limits
items.description: Even with external entities off, recursive internal entity expansion can exhaust memory (billion laughs / quadratic blowup).
items.how_to_find:
Check for billion-laughs / entity-expansion protection:

```
grep -REn 'entityExpansionLimit|FEATURE_SECURE_PROCESSING|maxEntity' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable secure processing / entity-expansion limits, cap document/entity size, and reject deeply nested XML. Re-review limits.

references.title: CWE-776: Improper Restriction of Recursive Entity References

items.title: Check XML-adjacent formats (SVG, DOCX, SAML)
items.description: SVG, OOXML documents, and SAML assertions are XML under the hood and inherit XXE risk when parsed unsafely.
items.how_to_find:
Check XML-based formats that also parse entities (SVG uploads, OOXML, SAML assertions):

```
grep -REn 'svg|docx|xlsx|SAML|assertion' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Harden XML parsing for these formats too (disable DTDs/entities), sanitize SVG, and validate SAML signatures + parser config. Re-review.

references.title: CWE-611: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference

items.title: Scan dependencies for known vulnerabilities
items.description: Third-party libraries with published CVEs are a top breach vector; every dependency tree should be scanned.
items.how_to_find:
Run an SCA scan:

```
# npm audit ; pip-audit ; osv-scanner -r . ; trivy fs . ; snyk test
osv-scanner -r .
```
items.how_to_fix: Fix/upgrade vulnerable dependencies (especially high/critical), automate SCA in CI, and use Dependabot/Renovate for updates. Re-scan.

references.title: OWASP Vulnerable Dependency Management Cheat Sheet
CWE-1395: Dependency on Vulnerable Third-Party Component

items.title: Verify lockfiles pin and integrity-check dependencies
items.description: Without a committed lockfile with integrity hashes, builds can pull in unexpected or tampered versions.
items.how_to_find:
Check for committed lockfiles with integrity hashes:

```
ls package-lock.json yarn.lock poetry.lock Pipfile.lock go.sum 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Commit lockfiles, install with `--frozen`/`npm ci` (verify integrity hashes), and pin versions. Re-verify reproducible installs.

references.title: OWASP Vulnerable Dependency Management Cheat Sheet
CWE-1357: Reliance on Insufficiently Trustworthy Component

items.title: Check for typosquatting and untrusted sources
items.description: Dependencies from unofficial registries, suspicious names, or install scripts can introduce malicious code.
items.how_to_find:
Review dependency names/sources for typosquats and non-official registries:

```
grep -REn 'registry|repository|source' package.json requirements*.txt pom.xml
```
items.how_to_fix: Verify package names/owners, use a private registry/proxy with allowlisting, enable scoped packages, and review new dependencies. Re-audit sources.

references.title: CWE-829: Inclusion of Functionality from Untrusted Control Sphere

items.title: Identify unmaintained or end-of-life dependencies
items.description: Abandoned libraries do not receive security fixes; depending on them is accumulating unpatched risk.
items.how_to_find:
Find stale/EOL dependencies (no recent releases, archived repos):

```
npm outdated ; pip list --outdated
```
items.how_to_fix: Replace unmaintained/EOL dependencies, reduce dependency count, and track maintenance status. Re-audit periodically.

references.title: OWASP Vulnerable Dependency Management Cheat Sheet
CWE-1104: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components

items.title: Detect debug mode enabled in production
items.description: Frameworks in debug mode (DEBUG=True, app.debug, development profile) expose stack traces, consoles, and secrets.
items.how_to_find:
Find debug flags:

```
grep -REn 'DEBUG\s*=\s*True|app.debug|NODE_ENV.*development|display_errors\s*=\s*On' src/ config/
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable debug in production (config per environment), fail the build if debug is on for prod, and remove debug endpoints. Re-verify prod config.

items.title: Check for verbose error and stack-trace exposure
items.description: Returning raw exceptions, stack traces, or SQL errors to clients leaks internal structure useful to attackers.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether errors leak stack traces/internal details to users:

```
grep -REn 'printStackTrace|traceback|e.getMessage\(\)|res.send\(err' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Return generic error messages to clients, log details server-side only, and add a global error handler. Re-review error handling.

references.title: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

items.title: Detect exposed admin and management endpoints
items.description: Actuator, metrics, GraphQL introspection, swagger, and admin panels left exposed leak data and attack surface.
items.how_to_find:
Find admin/actuator/debug endpoints:

```
grep -REn 'actuator|/admin|/debug|/metrics|/console|swagger' src/ config/
```
items.how_to_fix: Protect management endpoints with auth + network restrictions (internal only), disable those not needed, and don't expose them publicly. Re-test exposure.

items.title: Check security headers and CORS configuration
items.description: Missing CSP/HSTS or an overly permissive CORS policy (origin reflection with credentials) weakens browser protections.
items.how_to_find:
Review security headers + CORS:

```
grep -REn 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin|CORS|Content-Security-Policy|X-Frame-Options|HSTS' src/ config/
```

`Allow-Origin: *` with credentials = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set CSP, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options; restrict CORS to specific origins (never `*` with credentials). Re-review headers/CORS.

references.title: OWASP HTTP Security Response Headers Cheat Sheet
CWE-942: Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains

items.title: Detect prototype pollution in JavaScript
items.description: Recursive merge/clone/set on attacker-controlled keys (__proto__, constructor, prototype) can pollute Object.prototype.
items.how_to_find:
Find recursive merge/assign that can pollute `__proto__`:

```
grep -REn 'merge\(|extend\(|Object.assign\(|__proto__|constructor\[' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Use safe merge (reject `__proto__`/`constructor`/`prototype` keys), `Object.create(null)` for maps, `Map`, and validate input shapes. Re-review merge logic.

references.title: CWE-1321: Improperly Controlled Modification of Object Prototype

items.title: Detect Python pickle and unsafe dynamic features
items.description: Beyond pickle, Python footguns include eval/exec, yaml.load, and __reduce__ in custom classes used on untrusted data.
items.how_to_find:
Find unsafe Python features:

```
grep -REn 'pickle|eval\(|exec\(|__import__|input\(\)|subprocess.*shell=True' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Avoid pickle/eval/exec on untrusted data, use safe parsers, and validate input. Re-scan.

items.title: Detect PHP type juggling and loose comparison
items.description: Loose comparison (==) and functions like strcmp on arrays cause authentication and integrity bypasses in PHP.
items.how_to_find:
Find loose comparisons on security-sensitive values:

```
grep -REn '==[^=]|!=[^=]|in_array\([^,]+,[^,]+\)|strcmp' src/
```

`==` on hashes/tokens enables type-juggling bypass.
items.how_to_fix: Use strict comparison (`===`), `hash_equals()` for secrets, and strict `in_array` (third arg true). Re-review comparisons.

items.title: Detect unsafe Go patterns
items.description: Go footguns include text/template (vs html/template) for HTML, ignored errors, and the unsafe package bypassing safety.
items.how_to_find:
Find unsafe Go usage:

```
grep -REn 'unsafe\.|exec.Command\(.*\+|fmt.Sprintf.*SELECT|math/rand|InsecureSkipVerify' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Avoid `unsafe`, use argv `exec.Command` (no shell string), parameterize SQL, use `crypto/rand` for secrets, and check every error. Re-scan.

items.title: Audit unsafe blocks and panics in Rust
items.description: unsafe blocks bypass memory safety, and unwrap/expect/panic on untrusted input can be a DoS vector.
items.how_to_find:
Find `unsafe` blocks and panic-prone calls:

```
grep -REn 'unsafe\s*\{|unwrap\(\)|expect\(|transmute|from_raw' src/
```
items.how_to_fix: Minimize/justify `unsafe` (document invariants), handle `Result`/`Option` instead of `unwrap`/`expect` on untrusted input, and audit FFI boundaries. Re-review unsafe code.
